content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 25 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 112r | **Source Images:** DEU_025_page_001.jpg; DEU_025_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** When people have a dispute and bring it to court, the judges will hear the case and decide — acquitting the innocent and condemning the guilty. **2** If the guilty party deserves to be beaten, the judge shall have him lie down and be flogged in his presence with a number of lashes proportionate to his offense. **3** Forty lashes may be given, but no more. If more blows than that are administered, your fellow Israelite will be publicly degraded in your eyes.

**4** Do not muzzle an ox while it is treading out the grain.

**5** When brothers are living together and one of them dies without leaving a son, his widow must not marry outside the family. Her husband's brother shall take her as his wife and fulfill the duty of a brother-in-law to her. **6** The first son she bears will carry on the name of the dead brother so that his name will not be blotted out from Israel.

**7** But if the man does not want to marry his brother's widow, she shall go to the elders at the town gate and say, "My brother-in-law refuses to carry on his brother's name in Israel. He will not do his duty as my brother-in-law." **8** Then the elders of his town shall summon him and talk with him. If he persists and says, "I don't want to marry her," **9** his brother's widow shall approach him in the presence of the elders, remove his sandal from his foot, spit in his face, and declare: "This is what is done to the man who will not keep his brother's family line alive." **10** That man's family will be known in Israel as the House of the Sandal-Removed.

**11** If two men are fighting and the wife of one of them comes to rescue her husband from his attacker, and she reaches out and seizes him by his private parts, **12** you shall cut off her hand. Show her no pity.

**13** Do not carry two different weights in your bag — one heavy and one light. **14** Do not keep two different measuring containers in your house — one larger and one smaller. **15** You must have accurate and honest weights and measures, so that you may live long in the land the LORD your God is giving you. **16** For everyone who acts dishonestly in these matters is detestable to the LORD your God.

**17** Remember what the Amalekites did to you along the way when you came out of Egypt. **18** When you were exhausted and worn out, they met you on the road and attacked all who were lagging behind; they had no fear of God. **19** When the LORD your God gives you rest from all the enemies around you in the land he is giving you to possess as your inheritance, you shall blot out the name of Amalek from under heaven. Do not forget!

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verses 1-3 — Flogging with dignity:** The lashes cap at forty to prevent dehumanization of the guilty. PNEUMA renders "your brother" as "your fellow Israelite" to communicate the covenantal solidarity the Hebrew implies — the guilty man is still a member of the community.

**Verse 4 — The ox proverb:** This brief law functions as a principle of just compensation for labor. Its placement between human-dignity laws and levirate law is deliberate in the Hebrew — all three concern treating persons (and working creatures) as bearers of dignity, not merely instruments.

**Verses 5-10 — Levirate marriage (yibbum/chalitzah):** PNEUMA renders yibbum and chalitzah functionally throughout. The "brother-in-law duty" captures the social obligation without requiring the reader to know the technical terms. The sandal-removal ceremony (chalitzah) is rendered in full dramatic sequence to preserve the public, shaming dimension. "House of the Sandal-Removed" preserves the lasting social consequence of refusal.

**Verses 13-16 — Honest weights:** "Two different weights... one heavy and one light" — the Hebrew uses "a stone and a stone, a measure and a measure," meaning one honest and one dishonest. PNEUMA unpacks this idiom directly. "Detestable to the LORD" (to'evat YHWH) is the strongest condemnation formula in Deuteronomy; PNEUMA preserves its full force.

**Verses 17-19 — Remember Amalek:** The command timcheh et-zekher Amaleq ("blot out the memory/name of Amalek") closes the chapter with a jarring imperative. PNEUMA renders this literally but contextualizes the Amalekite offense — attacking the exhausted stragglers, and doing so "without fear of God." The final "Do not forget!" renders lo tishkach as a standalone emphatic, as the Hebrew intends.

— PNEUMA
